---
title: "Reinforcement Learning (RL) Guide"
format: html
---

*Adapted from the [Unsloth RL guide](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide).*

Reinforcement Learning is where an "agent" learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment and receiving **feedback** in the form of **rewards** or **penalties**.

* **Action:** What the model generates (e.g. a sentence).
* **Reward:** A signal indicating how good or bad the model's action was (e.g. did the response follow instructions? was it helpful?).
* **Environment:** The scenario or task the model is working on (e.g. answering a user's question).

### What you will learn

1. What is RL? RLVR? PPO? GRPO? RLHF? RFT? Is **"Luck is All You Need?"** for RL?
2. What is an environment? Agent? Action? Reward function? Rewards?

This article covers everything (from beginner to advanced) you need to know about GRPO, Reinforcement Learning (RL) and reward functions, along with tips, and the basics of using GRPO with [Unsloth](https://github.com/unslothai/unsloth). If you're looking for a step-by-step tutorial for using GRPO, see the Unsloth guide [Train your own reasoning model with GRPO](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/tutorial-train-your-own-reasoning-model-with-grpo).

::: {.callout-tip}
## Updates

**Jan 15, 2026:** [Ultra long context RL](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/grpo-long-context) is here. Train gpt-oss with a 380K context window.

**Nov 26, 2025:** [FP8 precision RL and GRPO](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/fp8-reinforcement-learning) in Unsloth.
:::

## What is Reinforcement Learning (RL)?

The goal of RL is to:

1. **Increase the chance of seeing "good" outcomes.**
2. **Decrease the chance of seeing "bad" outcomes.**

**That's it!** There are intricacies on what "good" and "bad" means, or how we go about "increasing" or "decreasing" it, or what even "outcomes" means.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
For example, in the **Pacman game**:

1. The **environment** is the game world.
2. The **actions** you can take are UP, LEFT, RIGHT and DOWN.
3. The **rewards** are good if you eat a cookie, or bad if you hit one of the squiggly enemies.
4. In RL, you can't know the "best action" you can take, but you can observe intermediate steps, or the final game state (win or lose)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-e853f7e6da505ee587642314b98180ebf840252c%2FRL%20Game.png?alt=media){fig-alt="Pacman-style RL diagram"}
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-30bade1550c877bb7f79075c80ac79476b0ecd76%2FMath%20RL.png?alt=media){fig-alt="Math question as RL"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Another example: you are given the question **"What is 2 + 2?"** (answer 4). An unaligned language model might output 3, 4, C, D, -10, literally anything.

1. Numbers are better than C or D, right?
2. Getting 3 is better than 8, right?
3. Getting 4 is definitely correct.

You have just designed a **reward function**!
:::

::::

### From RLHF, PPO to GRPO and RLVR

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-5d0c90e4b45507d3e12c8b938cbd1679cd38f4f9%2FRLHF.png?alt=media){fig-alt="RLHF diagram"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
OpenAI popularized [RLHF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinforcement_learning_from_human_feedback) (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), where we train an **agent** to produce outputs to a question (the **state**) that are rated more useful by humans.

The thumbs up and down in ChatGPT, for example, can be used in the RLHF process.
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-1e1dff9c921e787e669dee79c41a76db89e882e7%2FPPO.png?alt=media){fig-alt="PPO diagram"}

![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-f6156f2c519baf81e6ef286476f4092037303799%2FPPO%20formula.png?alt=media){fig-alt="PPO formula" fig-cap="PPO formula"}

The clip(..., 1-e, 1+e) term is used to force PPO not to take too large changes. There is also a KL term with beta set to > 0 to force the model not to deviate too much from the reference policy.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
To do RLHF, [**PPO**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proximal_policy_optimization) (Proximal Policy Optimization) was developed. The **agent** is the language model in this case. In fact it is composed of three systems:

1. The **generating policy** (current trained model)
2. The **reference policy** (original model)
3. The **value model** (average reward estimator)

The **reward model** scores the current completion, and the goal is to **maximize** that signal.

The formula for PPO looks complicated because it was designed to be stable. See the [Unsloth AI Engineer 2025 talk](https://docs.unsloth.ai/ai-engineers-2025) for deeper math on PPO.
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-4f4e188edbcad4f53aaa4a626bc5b2fd01334574%2FGRPO%20%2B%20RLVR.png?alt=media){fig-alt="GRPO and RLVR"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
DeepSeek developed [**GRPO**](https://unsloth.ai/blog/grpo) (Group Relative Policy Optimization) to train their R1 reasoning models. The key differences from PPO are:

1. The **value model is removed**, replaced with statistics from multiple samples.
2. The **separate reward model** can be replaced with a **custom reward function**, as in **RLVR**.
:::

::::

This means GRPO is extremely efficient. Previously PPO needed to train multiple models; with the value model and (often) the learned reward model out of the picture, you save memory and speed things up.

**RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards)** lets you reward the model on tasks with easy-to-verify solutions. For example:

1. Math equations can be verified (e.g. 2+2 = 4).
2. Code output can be verified by execution.
3. Designing verifiable reward functions can be hard, so many examples are math or code.
4. GRPO is not only for code or math—reasoning can help email automation, retrieval, law, and medicine, depending on your data and reward. The trick is to define a **rubric**: a list of smaller verifiable rewards, not one monolithic score. OpenAI describes a related idea in [reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT)](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reinforcement-fine-tuning).

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Why "group relative"?**

GRPO removes the value model entirely, but you still need an estimate of **average reward** given the current state.

The trick is to **sample the LLM** multiple times, then estimate average reward from those samples across prompts.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-29e188e5adc6de1e62c841e6cd9e34a2dae4994a%2FGroup%20Relative.png?alt=media){fig-alt="Group relative illustration"}
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
For "What is 2+2?" you might sample four answers: 4, 3, D, C. You score each, then compute **mean** and **standard deviation**, then **z-score** standardize.

That produces **advantages A**, which replace the value model and save a lot of memory.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-d40a73cd48b05b9205810a1946f4fc1dce81ae7d%2FStatistics.png?alt=media){fig-alt="GRPO advantage from statistics" fig-cap="GRPO advantage calculation"}
:::

::::

### Luck (well patience) is all you need

RL needs only two things:

1. A question or instruction (e.g. "What is 2+2?", "Create a Flappy Bird game in Python").
2. A reward function and verifier to judge whether the output is good or bad.

With those, you can **sample the model many times** until a good answer appears. For "What is 2+2?", a weak model might emit junk until it occasionally outputs **4** and gets a high reward.

So by chance, RL can find the correct answer across many **rollouts**. The goal is to make **4** much more likely and junk much less likely.

**In the limit, if the probability of the correct answer is small but non-zero, repeated sampling will eventually hit it**—hence the phrase **"luck is all you need"**; **"patience is all you need"** is the more sober version.

![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-4f0cb4803aa22583e88dfa8de8061b66bbe6a6b1%2FLuck%20is%20all%20you%20need.png?alt=media){width="375" fig-alt="Luck is all you need"}

RL does not only wait for lucky samples: **bad samples provide negative signal**, so the policy is nudged away from bad completions. Over time you **reshape** the output distribution toward the correct region, which makes learning **efficient**.

::: {.callout-warning}
If the probability of any useful completion is **always zero**, RL cannot help. That is one reason people start from an **instruction-tuned** model so the policy already has some chance of valid behavior.
:::

## What Unsloth offers for RL

* With 15GB VRAM, Unsloth allows you to turn many models up to ~17B parameters (e.g. Llama 3.1 8B, Phi-4 14B, Mistral 7B, Qwen2.5 7B) into reasoning-style models.
* **RL for vision / multimodal** is supported—see [Vision reinforcement learning (VLM RL)](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/vision-reinforcement-learning-vlm-rl).
* **Rough minimum:** about **5GB VRAM** for local reasoning-style training on small models (about 1.5B parameters or less).

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
- [Tutorial: train your own reasoning model with GRPO](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/tutorial-train-your-own-reasoning-model-with-grpo)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- [Vision reinforcement learning (VLM RL)](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/vision-reinforcement-learning-vlm-rl)
:::

::::

::: {.callout-note}
For **advanced GRPO** (batching, generation, training parameters), see the [Advanced RL documentation](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/advanced-rl-documentation).
:::

### GRPO notebooks {#grpo-notebooks}

| [Qwen3.5 (4B) Vision — new](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_5_(4B)_Vision_GRPO.ipynb) | [Qwen3-VL-8B Vision GSPO](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_VL_(8B)-Vision-GRPO.ipynb) | [Gemma 3 (4B) Vision GSPO](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Gemma3_(4B)-Vision-GRPO.ipynb) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [gpt-oss-20b GSPO](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/gpt-oss-(20B)-GRPO.ipynb) | [DeepSeek-R1-0528-Qwen3-8B](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/DeepSeek_R1_0528_Qwen3_(8B)_GRPO.ipynb) | [Llama 3.2 (3B) Advanced](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Advanced_Llama3_2_(3B)_GRPO_LoRA.ipynb) |
| [Qwen3 (4B) Advanced](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_(4B)-GRPO.ipynb) | [Phi-4 (14B)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Phi_4_(14B)-GRPO.ipynb) | [Qwen2.5 (3B)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen2.5_(3B)-GRPO.ipynb) |
| [Mistral v0.3 (7B)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Mistral_v0.3_(7B)-GRPO.ipynb) | [Llama 3.1 (8B)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Llama3.1_(8B)-GRPO.ipynb) | [Qwen3-8B FP8 (L4)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_8B_FP8_GRPO.ipynb) |

::: {.callout-tip}
Unsloth supports [**GSPO**](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/advanced-rl-documentation/gspo-reinforcement-learning) and related variants. Example `GRPOConfig` knobs:

```python
epsilon=0.2,
epsilon_high=0.28,  # one sided
delta=1.5  # two sided

loss_type='gspo',
# or:
loss_type='grpo',
# or:
loss_type='dr_grpo',

mask_truncated_completions=True,
```
:::

* If you see no reasoning, check training length and that your [reward function / verifier](#reward-functions-verifier) is wired correctly; see [reward function examples](#reward-function-examples).
* Earlier demos used large GPUs for small models; Unsloth targets much lower VRAM for similar ideas.
* GRPO works with **QLoRA and LoRA**, not only full fine-tuning.
* For [**20K context**](#grpo-requirement-guidelines) with eight generations per prompt, Unsloth reports far lower VRAM than a standard FA2-style stack for Llama 3.1 8B.
* This is about **training** a model with GRPO, not only using R1-distilled checkpoints.

![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-5ae836156344a7c22241d0f76dbea09d58e04f8f%2Fprompt%20only%20example.png?alt=media){fig-alt="Phi-4 with and without GRPO thinking token"}

## Training with GRPO

Step-by-step: [Train your own reasoning model with GRPO](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/tutorial-train-your-own-reasoning-model-with-grpo).

::: {.callout-tip}
Advanced topics: [Advanced RL documentation](https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide/advanced-rl-documentation).
:::

### How GRPO trains a model

1. For each prompt, the model generates several completions (e.g. eight).
2. Each completion is scored with your reward functions.
3. **Steps:** with 300 rows, one pass is ~300 optimizer steps (× epochs if you repeat data). You can raise the number of samples per prompt (e.g. 8 → 16).
4. Weights update every step from the GRPO objective.

::: {.callout-warning}
If learning stalls, try the [Advanced GRPO notebooks](https://unsloth.ai/docs/unsloth-notebooks#grpo-reasoning-notebooks)—they use stronger reward shaping.
:::

### Basics and tips

* Wait at least **~300 steps** before expecting clear reward trends; long runs (on the order of **hours**) are common.
* **500+ prompts** is a better default than tiny sets (though you can experiment with tens).
* Runs differ by model, data, and rewards; some setups need **1000+** steps.
* Local Unsloth: install **`diffusers`** if prompted; keep **vLLM** current.
* Prefer models **≥ ~1.5B** parameters if you want reliable “thinking”-style behavior.
* VRAM rule of thumb for **4-bit QLoRA**: model size in billions ≈ minimum practical VRAM (context and LoRA rank increase this). **16-bit LoRA** needs several times more.
* **Continuous training** is fine—GRPO can run in the background.
* Example notebooks often use [**GSM8K**](#gsm8k-reward-functions)-style rewards.
* **Chat templates** matter if you start from a base model.
* Reward vs. step should trend up over time when the setup is healthy.

![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-e44683faa4765a3b803edd4c02c4b468e45cc91d%2Funnamed.png?alt=media){width="563" fig-alt="Reward vs steps"}

Training logs for GRPO (including per–reward-function breakdowns) are built into Unsloth.

![](https://3215535692-files.gitbook.io/~/files/v0/b/gitbook-x-prod.appspot.com/o/spaces%2FxhOjnexMCB3dmuQFQ2Zq%2Fuploads%2Fgit-blob-58d958e1a3bfd968f1b1a4995a28261aa6413337%2FScreenshot%202025-02-20%20at%2004-52-52%20Copy%20of%20Yet%20another%20copy%20of%20Llama3.1_(8B)-GRPO.ipynb%20-%20Colab.png?alt=media){fig-alt="Unsloth GRPO logging UI"}

### RL on models without vLLM

You can run RL on models that vLLM does not support yet (e.g. [Qwen3.5](https://unsloth.ai/docs/models/qwen3.5/fine-tune)) by disabling fast inference:

```python
from unsloth import FastLanguageModel

model, tokenizer = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained(
    model_name="unsloth/Qwen3.5-4B",
    fast_inference=False,
)
```

## Reward functions / verifiers {#reward-functions-verifier}

In RL, a **reward function** and a **verifier** are related but not identical:

**Verifier**

* Decides whether an output is correct (boolean / discrete check), often without a numeric score.
* Example: answer "5" for 2+2 is **wrong**.
* Can run code (e.g. execute a program) to check behavior.

**Reward function**

* Maps criteria (including verifier output) to a **scalar** score.
* Example: wrong → −3, correct → +3; optional penalties for length or style.

**Summary:** verifiers **check**; reward functions **score**. A reward function often **calls** a verifier.

### Understanding reward functions

* GRPO **updates weights** to increase expected reward—not only next-token likelihood.
* **SFT** optimizes likelihood; GRPO optimizes your **task metric** (via rewards).
* You can **reuse** the same prompts across epochs.
* Rewards can be hand-coded, learned, or hybrid; **bad rewards** can hurt you as much as good ones help.

### Reward function examples {#reward-function-examples}

You can prototype rewards by critiquing samples with another model (“too robotic → −3”, etc.).

#### Example 1: simple arithmetic

* **Question:** `2 + 2`
* **Answer:** `4`
* **Reward 1:** number present → +1; no number → −1
* **Reward 2:** matches gold → +3; else → −3
* **Total:** sum of terms

#### Example 2: email automation

* Keyword present → +1
* Matches gold template → +1
* Too long → −1
* Uses recipient name → +1
* Signature block present → +1

### Unsloth proximity-based rewards

The [advanced GRPO notebooks](#grpo-notebooks) include a **proximity** reward: closer numeric (or structured) answers score higher than wild guesses.

* Reasoning templates beyond a bare `think` tag (e.g. custom XML segments)
* Regex checks and **pre-finetuning** to avoid “format-only” hacking
* **Graded** scores instead of a single all-or-nothing gate where it helps

#### GSM8K-style helpers {#gsm8k-reward-functions}

Examples often reuse GSM8K reward snippets (e.g. by [@willccbb](https://x.com/willccbb)):

* **correctness_reward_func** — exact match to label
* **int_reward_func** — prefer integer answers
* **soft_format_reward_func** / **strict_format_reward_func** — structure checks
* **xmlcount_reward_func** — tag balance / count constraints

## Using vLLM

[vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/) can sit in the same stack for **fast generation** during training. Throughput depends on GPU; Unsloth’s integration avoids **duplicate** weight copies when both Unsloth and vLLM are loaded (meaningful VRAM savings on larger models).

Enable fast inference after installing vLLM:

```python
# pip install unsloth vllm
from unsloth import FastLanguageModel
model, tokenizer = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained(
    model_name="unsloth/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct",
    fast_inference=True,
)
```

## GRPO requirement guidelines {#grpo-requirement-guidelines}

Unsloth applies several tricks (efficient linear kernels, gradient checkpointing, shared GPU memory with the inference engine, etc.) so **long-context GRPO** is far cheaper than a naive FA2 baseline for the same model width.

1. **VRAM (4-bit QLoRA):** very rough rule—**billions of parameters ≈ minimum VRAM in GB** before headroom for context and batching. More context and **16-bit LoRA** scale this up.
2. **Kernels + checkpointing** reduce activation memory on long sequences.
3. **Shared vLLM / training memory** avoids loading two full copies of weights.

| Metric | Unsloth | Standard + FA2 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Training memory (GB) | 42 | 414 |
| GRPO memory (GB) | 9.8 | 78.3 |
| Inference weights (GB) | 0 | 16 |
| KV cache 20K ctx (GB) | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| **Total (example)** | **54.3** | **510.8** |

Naive GRPO can materialize huge logit tensors; Unsloth’s path is aimed at **long context without** those multi-hundred-GB peaks.

The **KV cache** (16-bit) for Llama 3.1 8B at 20K tokens is on the order of **2.5GB** per batch in the illustrative table; batching vLLM increases KV needs proportionally.

## Unsloth RL workshop (video)

```{=html}
<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/OkEGJ5G3foU" title="Unsloth RL workshop" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```

## Further reading

1. Nathan Lambert — [RLHF book, policy gradients](https://rlhfbook.com/c/11-policy-gradients.html)
2. Yannic Kilcher — [GRPO video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bAWV_yrqx4w)
3. [AI Engineer 2025 materials (Unsloth)](https://docs.unsloth.ai/ai-engineers-2025)
4. [Advanced GRPO notebook (docs)](https://docs.unsloth.ai/basics/reinforcement-learning-guide/tutorial-train-your-own-reasoning-model-with-grpo)
5. [Qwen3 (4B) GRPO from base (Colab)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_(4B)-GRPO.ipynb)

## More video tutorials

```{=html}
<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/9t-BAjzBWj8" title="RL / GRPO tutorial" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=html}
<iframe width="100%" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/bbFEYPx9Hpo?start=3289" title="Dataset prep and RL + GRPO" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=html}
<iframe width="100%" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/oF0_eMhzRaQ" title="RL tutorial" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```
:::

::::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=html}
<iframe width="100%" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/juOh1afy-IE" title="GRPO tutorial" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{=html}
<iframe width="100%" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/SoPE1cUz3Hs" title="Local GRPO" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen></iframe>
```
:::

::::

---

References:

- https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide
